items.q: In an exothermic reaction, what happens energetically with the bonds?
items.correct: Energy released forming new bonds exceeds energy absorbed breaking existing bonds.
items.misconception: Bond breaking releases energy and bond making absorbs energy.
items.random_wrong: The reactants get colder because they lose mass as energy.
items.why: Students confuse the direction: they think breaking is 'releasing' (like cracking something open releases contents), when actually breaking always requires energy input.

items.q: Why can an endothermic reaction still be spontaneous?
items.correct: If the entropy increase (TΔS) is large enough to outweigh the positive ΔH, ΔG can still be negative.
items.misconception: Endothermic reactions are never spontaneous because they require energy input.
items.random_wrong: All endothermic reactions are spontaneous at room temperature.
items.why: Students equate 'exothermic' with 'spontaneous' from GCSE, missing the role of entropy in Gibbs free energy.

items.q: Why is the hydration enthalpy of a small, highly charged ion very exothermic?
items.correct: Small/highly charged ions form strong ion-dipole attractions with water molecules, releasing a large amount of energy.
items.misconception: Hydration enthalpy only depends on the size of the ion, not its charge.
items.random_wrong: Hydration enthalpy is the same for all ions because water molecules are identical.
items.why: Students focus on one factor (size OR charge) when actually both matter — charge density determines the strength of ion-dipole interactions.

items.q: ΔHf of O2(g) in standard state?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 0. “+200” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Products of complete combustion of hydrocarbon?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: CO2 + H2O. “CO + H2” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
The real figure is -890. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
